title: Use methods and techniques of formalization and algorithmization of software program-code tasks
glossary: Abstraction
The principle of hiding implementation details and exposing only the essential properties and behavior of a data model or algorithm, so that complex problems can be reasoned about at a higher level.
Algorithm
A finite, ordered sequence of unambiguous instructions that transforms input data into a required result in a finite number of steps.
Big-O notation
A mathematical notation that describes the asymptotic upper bound of an algorithm's running time or memory use as a function of the input size n.
Binary search
A search algorithm that repeatedly halves a sorted array to locate a target value in O(log n) time.
Data structure
A way of organizing and storing data in memory (array, list, stack, queue, tree, graph, hash table) so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently.
Dynamic programming
A method of solving problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems, solving each once and storing the results to avoid recomputation.
Graph
A data structure consisting of a set of vertices (nodes) and a set of edges connecting them, used to model relationships and networks.
Hash table
A data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function, providing average O(1) insertion, deletion and lookup.
Heap
A specialized tree-based structure that satisfies the heap property and provides efficient access to the minimum or maximum element; the basis of a priority queue.
Priority queue
An abstract data type in which each element has a priority and elements are served in order of priority rather than order of arrival.
Recursion
A technique in which a function solves a problem by calling itself on smaller instances of the same problem until a base case is reached.
Stack
A last-in, first-out (LIFO) linear data structure that supports push and pop operations at one end.
Tree
A hierarchical data structure of nodes with a single root, in which each node has zero or more child nodes and there are no cycles.
